modules.resources:
Additional Resources — Who Pays for Healthcare?

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • Parts of Medicare — Medicare Part A covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, hospice care, and some home health services. https://www.medicare.gov/basics/get-started-with-medicare/medicare-basics/parts-of-medicare
  • Original Medicare (Part A and B) Eligibility and Enrollment — People are eligible for Medicare when they turn 65. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/enrollment-renewal/original-part-a-b
  • 2026 Medicare Parts A & B Premiums and Deductibles — Most people do not pay a premium for Part A if they have 40 or more quarters of Medicare-covered employment. https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/2026-medicare-parts-b-premiums-deductibles
  • Medicaid — Medicaid provides health coverage to eligible low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly adults, and people with disabilities. https://www.medicaid.gov/medicaid
  • Types of health insurance — Health insurance falls into three broad categories: employer-sponsored, individual/marketplace, and government programs. https://www.cms.gov/medical-bill-rights/help/guides/what-type-of-insurance
  • Health insurance plan & network types: HMOs, PPOs, and more — Common commercial plan types include HMO, PPO, EPO, and POS. https://www.healthcare.gov/choose-a-plan/plan-types/
  • Covered Entities and Business Associates — Health plans are one of the three types of HIPAA covered entities, alongside health care providers and clearinghouses. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/covered-entities/index.html
  • Are You a Covered Entity? — HIPAA covered entities include health plans, healthcare providers who transmit electronically, and clearinghouses. https://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/burden-reduction/administrative-simplification/hipaa/covered-entities
  • Know your rights without insurance — Self-pay patients are those who do not have insurance or choose not to use their insurance for a given service. https://www.cms.gov/medical-bill-rights/know-your-rights/no-insurance
  • Medicare Secondary Payer — A payer is an entity responsible for paying healthcare providers, including private insurers and government programs. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/coordination-benefits-recovery/overview/secondary-payer
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — Who Pays for Healthcare?

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. For three people — a 70-year-old retiree, a low-income parent of two, and a salaried software engineer — decide which payor category (government, commercial, or self-pay) most likely covers each, and justify your choice.
  2. A patient has both an employer plan and Medicare. Explain coordination of benefits: which is the primary payer, and what does the secondary payer do?

modules.resources:
Additional Resources — Who Provides Healthcare?

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • CMS Glossary – Health Care Provider — A health care provider is a person who is trained and licensed to give health care, or a place that is licensed to give health care. https://www.cms.gov/glossary?items_per_page=10&term=provider&viewmode=grid
  • Become a Medicare Provider or Supplier — Medicare distinguishes between providers (institutional care facilities like hospitals) and suppliers (entities furnishing diagnostic, therapeutic, or equipment services). https://www.cms.gov/medicare/enrollment-renewal/providers-suppliers
  • CMS Enrollment Applications (CMS-855 Forms) — CMS-855A is for institutional providers (hospitals, SNFs, home health agencies, hospices). https://www.cms.gov/medicare/enrollment-renewal/providers-suppliers/chain-ownership-system-pecos/enrollment-applications
  • Enroll as a DMEPOS Supplier — DME is equipment that can withstand repeated use, has an expected life of at least 3 years, serves a medical purpose, and is appropriate for home use. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/enrollment-renewal/providers-suppliers/durable-medical-equipment-prosthetics-orthotics-supplies-dmepos
  • Covered Entities and Business Associates — HIPAA covered entities include health care clearinghouses, health plans, and health care providers who conduct certain electronic transactions. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/covered-entities/index.html
  • Professional Paper Claim Form (CMS-1500) — The CMS-1500 form is the standard claim form used by non-institutional providers and suppliers to bill Medicare. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/billing/electronicbillingeditrans/1500
  • Institutional Paper Claim Form (CMS-1450 / UB-04) — The CMS-1450 form (also known as UB-04) is used by institutional providers (hospitals, SNFs, home health agencies) to bill Medicare. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/coding-billing/electronic-billing/institutional-paper-claim-form
  • Ambulance Services Coverage — Medicare covers ambulance services only when the patient's medical condition makes other transportation contraindicated. https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/ambulance-services
  • Medicaid Provider Enrollment Requirements Fact Sheet — States must have an agreement between the Medicaid agency and each provider furnishing services. https://www.cms.gov/files/document/mpe-factsheet082616pdf
  • CMS Provider of Services File – Clinical Laboratories — Clinical laboratories exist in many facility types including hospitals, ambulatory surgery centers, physician offices, pharmacies, and independent labs. https://data.cms.gov/provider-characteristics/hospitals-and-other-facilities/provider-of-services-file-clinical-laboratories
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — Who Provides Healthcare?

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. Classify five entities — a general hospital, a wheelchair supplier, an air ambulance, an independent lab, and a physician office — as a Medicare 'provider' or 'supplier', and name the claim form each uses (CMS-1500/837P vs CMS-1450/837I).
  2. Trace how a newly licensed physician enrolls to bill Medicare, naming each step (NPI → PECOS → MAC) and the form they file.

modules.resources:
Additional Resources — Who Receives Healthcare?

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • Glossary of Health Coverage and Medical Terms (Uniform Glossary) — A subscriber is the primary individual who enrolls in and holds the insurance plan contract with the carrier. https://www.cms.gov/CCIIO/Resources/Forms-Reports-and-Other-Resources/Downloads/Uniform-Glossary-01-2020.pdf
  • Dependent – HealthCare.gov Glossary — A dependent is a child or other individual for whom a parent, relative, or other person may claim a personal exemption tax deduction. https://www.healthcare.gov/glossary/dependent/
  • Coverage for Young Adults — The ACA requires health plans that offer dependent coverage to make it available until the child reaches age 26. https://www.cms.gov/marketplace/private-health-insurance/coverage-young-adults
  • Your Rights Under HIPAA — Patients have the right to ask to see or get a copy of their health and claims records. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-individuals/guidance-materials-for-consumers/index.html
  • Individuals' Right under HIPAA to Access their Health Information — The HIPAA Privacy Rule gives individuals a legal, enforceable right to see and receive copies of their health information. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/guidance/access/index.html
  • Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule — Protected health information (PHI) is individually identifiable health information relating to past, present, or future physical or mental health, provision of care, or payment for care. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html
  • Getting Health Coverage Outside Open Enrollment — Open Enrollment is the annual period when anyone can enroll in or change a Marketplace health plan. https://www.healthcare.gov/coverage-outside-open-enrollment/special-enrollment-period/
  • Your Total Costs for Health Care: Premium, Deductible, and Out-of-Pocket Costs — A premium is the amount you pay each month to maintain your health insurance coverage. https://www.healthcare.gov/choose-a-plan/your-total-costs/
  • Health Insurance Terms You Should Know — Cost sharing is the patient's share of costs for plan-covered services that must be paid out of pocket. https://www.cms.gov/medical-bill-rights/help/guides/health-insurance-terms
  • Informed Consent – StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf) — Informed consent is a communication process in which a clinician educates a patient about the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a procedure or intervention. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430827/
  • Patient Engagement – CDC Health Literacy — Patient activation is the knowledge, skills, and confidence to become actively engaged in one's health care. https://www.cdc.gov/health-literacy/php/research-summaries/patient-engagement.html
  • How to Read an Explanation of Benefits — An EOB shows the costs of care, including services received, dates, amount the plan pays, and the amount the patient owes. https://www.cms.gov/medical-bill-rights/help/guides/explanation-of-benefits
  • Notice of Privacy Practices — The HIPAA Privacy Rule requires covered entities to provide a Notice of Privacy Practices in plain language. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-individuals/notice-privacy-practices/index.html
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — Who Receives Healthcare?

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. A 24-year-old is covered under a parent's plan. Identify who is the subscriber and who is the dependent, and list two life events that would open a Special Enrollment Period for them.
  2. Given a plan with a $1,000 deductible, 20% coinsurance, and a $6,000 out-of-pocket maximum, work out what the patient pays for a single $3,000 covered procedure (assume the deductible is not yet met).

modules.resources:
Additional Resources — Healthcare Data and Privacy

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • What is PHI? — PHI is individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by a covered entity or its business associate, in any form or medium. https://www.hhs.gov/answers/hipaa/what-is-phi/index.html
  • Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule — The Privacy Rule protects all individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by a covered entity or its business associate. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html
  • Summary of the HIPAA Security Rule — The Security Rule establishes national standards to protect electronic protected health information (ePHI). https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/laws-regulations/index.html
  • Breach Notification Rule — Covered entities must notify affected individuals of a breach of unsecured PHI without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/breach-notification/index.html
  • Covered Entities and Business Associates — HIPAA covered entities are health care clearinghouses, health plans, and health care providers who transmit health information electronically. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/covered-entities/index.html
  • ICD-10 — ICD-10-CM (Clinical Modification) is used by all providers in every healthcare setting for coding diagnoses. https://www.cms.gov/medicare/coding-billing/icd-10-codes
  • Transitioning to ICD-10 — ICD-9-CM had approximately 14,000 diagnosis codes; ICD-10-CM has approximately 69,000 diagnosis codes. https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/transitioning-icd-10
  • Overview of Coding & Classification Systems — HCPCS Level I consists of CPT codes — 5-digit numeric codes maintained by the AMA to identify medical services and procedures. https://www.cms.gov/cms-guide-medical-technology-companies-and-other-interested-parties/coding/overview-coding-classification-systems
  • Health Insurance Plan & Network Types — An in-network provider has a contract with a health insurer to provide services at agreed-upon rates, resulting in lower costs for the patient. https://www.healthcare.gov/choose-a-plan/plan-types/
  • Electronic Health Records — An EHR is an electronic version of a patient's medical history maintained by the provider over time. https://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/e-health/records
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — Healthcare Data and Privacy

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. Take a simple clinical scenario (e.g., a knee X-ray for knee pain) and identify which code types appear on the claim — the ICD-10-CM diagnosis and the CPT/HCPCS procedure — and explain why both are required.
  2. Decide whether each item is PHI: a patient's name alone, a lab result linked to a patient, and a de-identified count of flu cases. Name the HIPAA rule that governs PHI.

modules.resources:
Additional Resources — How It All Connects

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • Electronic Health Care Claims — Claims are electronically transmitted from providers to Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs) https://www.cms.gov/medicare/coding-billing/electronic-billing/electronic-healthcare-claims
  • Health Plan Eligibility Benefit Inquiry and Response — The eligibility/benefit inquiry transaction (270) obtains information about an enrollee's benefit plan including eligibility and coverage https://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/burden-reduction/administrative-simplification/transactions/health-plan-eligibility-benefit-inquiry-response
  • Are You a Covered Entity? — HIPAA covered entities are health care clearinghouses, certain health care providers, and health plans https://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/burden-reduction/administrative-simplification/hipaa/covered-entities
  • How to Read an Explanation of Benefits — An EOB is a document received from a health plan after a provider visit showing total charges and how much each party owes https://www.cms.gov/medical-bill-rights/help/guides/explanation-of-benefits
  • National Provider Identifier Standard (NPI) — The NPI is a unique 10-digit identification number for covered health care providers required by HIPAA https://www.cms.gov/regulations-and-guidance/administrative-simplification/nationalprovidentstand
  • Adopted Standards and Operating Rules — HIPAA mandates standard electronic transaction formats for the healthcare industry https://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/burden-reduction/administrative-simplification/hipaa/adopted-standards-operating-rules
  • Overview of Coding and Classification Systems — ICD-10-CM codes represent diagnoses and are used by all providers in every healthcare setting https://www.cms.gov/cms-guide-medical-technology-companies-and-other-interested-parties/coding/overview-coding-classification-systems
  • Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule — Protected health information (PHI) includes data relating to health condition, provision of health care, or payment for health care that identifies the individual https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html
  • Medicare Billing: 837P and Form CMS-1500 — The 837P is the HIPAA standard electronic format for professional healthcare claim submission https://www.cms.gov/files/document/mln006976-medicare-billing-cms-1500-837p.pdf
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — How It All Connects

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. Walk a claim end-to-end: eligibility check (270/271) → claim submission (837) → adjudication → remittance (835)/EOB. Identify one point where the claim could be rejected and one where it could be denied, and explain the difference.
  2. Explain how a clearinghouse and the National Provider Identifier (NPI) together let any provider submit a claim to any health plan.

modules.resources:
Additional Resources — Medicare vs. Medicaid: Telling Them Apart

Authoritative, approved sources (HHS / CMS / CDC / healthcare.gov and CMS-operated Medicare.gov & Medicaid.gov) that deepen this module. Each links to the primary source.

  • Your health plan options — Medicare Advantage (Part C) — Medicare Advantage (Part C) is a Medicare-approved plan from a private company that is an alternative to Original Medicare. https://www.medicare.gov/health-drug-plans/health-plans/your-health-plan-options
  • What's Medicare Drug Coverage (Part D)? — Medicare Part D is optional prescription drug coverage that helps pay for brand-name and generic drugs. https://www.medicare.gov/health-drug-plans/part-d
  • People Dually Eligible for Medicare and Medicaid (CMS MMCO Fact Sheet) — Some people qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid and are known as 'dually eligible' individuals. https://www.cms.gov/medicare-medicaid-coordination/medicare-and-medicaid-coordination/medicare-medicaid-coordination-office/downloads/mmco_factsheet.pdf
  • Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP) — CHIP provides low-cost health coverage to children in families that earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but cannot afford private coverage. https://www.medicaid.gov/chip/index.html
  • Long Term Services & Supports (Medicaid) — Medicaid is the primary payer for long-term services and supports (LTSS) in the United States, including nursing home care. https://www.medicaid.gov/medicaid/long-term-services-supports/index.html
modules.exercises:
Stretch & High-Impact Exercises — Medicare vs. Medicaid: Telling Them Apart

Apply this module's ideas to realistic situations. These are foundational — they reinforce the core distinctions rather than introduce advanced topics.

  1. For four people — a child in a family earning just above the Medicaid limit, a 70-year-old retiree, a low-income senior, and a low-income adult under 65 — decide whether each is best matched to Medicare, Medicaid, CHIP, or dual eligibility, and justify each.
  2. For a dual-eligible individual, explain which program pays first, which is the payer of last resort, and name a major category of care (long-term services and supports) that Medicaid covers but Original Medicare generally does not.
